key_insight: A machine-learning model can predict metallic glass critical cooling rates using a small set of elemental and simulation-derived features, with physically meaningful feature importance.
engineering_relevance: This provides a scalable way to screen alloy compositions for glass-forming ability and prioritize candidates for high-throughput materials design.
method: Combine one elemental descriptor (ideal entropy from stoichiometry) with three computed features from simulation/physics.
Use features from ab initio, machine-learning potential, and empirical potential molecular dynamics calculations.
Interpret predictions with Shapley additive explanations to verify physically reasonable feature influence.
evidence_signal: Across 34 alloys from 20 chemical systems, leave-one-chemical-system-out cross validation achieved R^2 = 0.78 and mean absolute error = 0.76 in log10(K/s).
watch_item: The model was evaluated on a relatively small set of alloys, so generalization to new chemistries still depends on the quality and consistency of the underlying simulated features.
paper_title: Machine learning metallic glass critical cooling rates through elemental and molecular simulation based featurization
